data.title: H.B. Fuller Just Got Leaner And Smarter, And That's Bullish
data.description:
Thesis

My research argues that H.B. Fuller Company (FUL) is executing a deliberate approach in favor of a tighter, higher-margin portfolio. The Q2 2025 results show that a shift taking shape, with the company steering its adhesives and sealants toward big-ticket trends like infrastructure upgrades, clean energy, and advanced materials, instead of chasing volume.

Highlights from H.B. Fuller's Q2 2025 Results

What looks like routine corporate shuffling at H.B. Fuller may actually be a calculated move, timed to catch a shift in the industry. The market seemed to think so, shares jumped nearly 11% after the company’s latest earnings report. Management, staring down a $80 billion global adhesives market, has decided the game of chasing every last dollar of volume isn’t worth it anymore. They're backing off low-margin areas and doubling down where profits are higher and future demand is more secure.

In December, H.B. Fuller dumped its Flooring business, a unit that pulled in $160 million a year but only managed $15 million in EBITDA. The $80 million sale price won’t turn heads, but that’s not the point. The company ditched a flatlining segment, clearing out financial clutter and sparing management the headache of chasing marginal gains.

Moreover, the cash arrives just as Fuller is pulling disparate product families like Insulated Glass, Woodworking, Composites, Roofing, Building Envelope, and Infrastructure into its new Building Adhesive Solutions unit. This group produced $850 million in net revenue and $130 million in adjusted EBITDA last year, implying an operating margin north of 15% out of the gate.

These product lines used to operate in their own silos, which meant little cross-selling and a lot of redundant development. Now, with Shahbaz Malik running the show, the bet is that shared customer lists can be squeezed for more without spending more. In theory, each new sales dollar drops harder to the bottom line.

Even so, the logic is not purely internal.

Global infrastructure build-outs, tighter building-envelope standards, and the green-energy push are setting up a multi-decade demand runway. Fuller wants its glue on every pane of insulated glass, every composite bridge deck, and every rooftop solar array.

The solar segment is where the strategy gets surgical. With labor making up more than half the cost of installation, contractors obsess over anything that saves time. Fuller’s pitch, products like EternaBond DoubleStick and Adcobond D18-221, combines sealing and flashing into one step. That means less time on ladders, fewer mistakes, and faster jobs. These may be just adhesives, but the impact isn’t small. The non-curing butyl formula stays tacky and watertight for decades, and by some measures beats standard MS-polymer products. Metal Construction Association data says it lasts at least 35 years, maybe up to 60.

A lot of the post-COVID hygiene habits haven’t gone anywhere either, so demand for top-shelf tissue and towel products is still strong. Fuller’s Swift tak line, less water, more solids, is built to help converters cut emissions, trim waste, and, with the help of auto-dilution, crank out up to 20% more product. The point is that these converters run on razor-thin margins, so any efficiency boost tends to stick. And that means that the supplier who delivers it usually does too. The division only posted 1.8% organic growth, not exactly impressive at first glance, but that was enough to lift the EBITDA margin by 300 basis points to 15.6%, a sign that price discipline and throughput benefits are flowing through.

Meanwhile, if geography is destiny, Fuller’s recent footprint expansion across India, the Middle East, and Africa underscores its intent to be present where urbanization is running hottest. The new factory in Cairo, built to LEED specs and certified under FSSC 22000 and SEDEX, gives Fuller a way to move products into Europe, North America, and Africa without waiting around. While that was going on, the expanded site in Ras Al-Khaimah, beefed up by the XCHEM deal, now supplies waterproofing, HVAC, and woodworking customers up and down the Gulf. Add it up, and Fuller’s IMEA footprint includes 5 plants, 450-plus employees, and exports to more than 100 countries. This gives Fuller local cost bases but global reach. Because many governments in the region are channeling petrodollar surpluses into infrastructure and housing, the addressable market is expanding even if global GDP cools elsewhere.

All the strategy talk doesn’t mean much without numbers to back it up. The latest quarter delivered some. Adjusted earnings per share reached $1.18, ten cents above expectations and up 5% from last year. Adjusted EBITDA rose to $166 million, also a 5% gain, with margins expanding 130 basis points to 18.4%. Gross margin bumped up to 32.2%, helped by higher-value products holding their price.

Segment performance mirrors the portfolio thesis. Engineering Adhesives posted a 24% EBITDA jump and a margin of 22.9%, even though revenue slipped 0.4%, with solar-related softness masking growth in automotive, aerospace, and defense.

The freshly minted BAS unit eked out a 5% EBITDA gain on a 0.9% sales decline, suggesting early cost synergy is offsetting near-term top-line noise. Hygiene, Health and Consumable, for its part, converted modest revenue expansion into solid profit momentum.

In the meantime, cash discipline has not been sacrificed to growth, as we see that operating cash flow jumped 36% from last year to $111 million, giving management enough room to buy back 300,000 shares in the quarter and 1 million so far this year. Net-debt-to-EBITDA edged down from 3.5x to 3.4x. It’s not a low number, but it’s moving in the right direction. With a free cash flow target of $300 to $325 million for 2025, there’s room to pay off debt or pick up smaller deals.

What’s more, Fuller just raised full-year EBITDA guidance to a range of $615 to $630 million, up 4 to 6%, while forecasting adjusted EPS between $4.10 and $4.30, a 7 to 12% lift. Even the third-quarter midpoint, $170 million of EBITDA, would keep margins humming if volume headwinds persist.

Headwinds, Outlook, and Valuation for H.B. Fuller

Next, let’s look at some bearish signals in Fuller’s second-quarter report, starting with revenue, which dropped to $898.1 million, $1.9 million short of Wall Street’s target and down 2.07% from last year. Strip out the Flooring unit they dumped late last year, and revenue technically rises 2.8%, but that’s less a rebound and more a company trying to stay upright while the floor moves under it. Organic growth, which filters out deals and currency volatility, came in at just 0.4%, and volumes slipped 0.3%.

Because the weakness didn't confine itself to one trouble spot, the segment breakdown reads like a map of overlapping stress points. Engineering Adhesives, which is usually a bright spot, absorbed another 80-basis-point margin hit from the chronically choppy solar market, and even though that drag is lighter than the 120-basis-point gouge recorded a year earlier, it still hurts.

Building Adhesive Solutions fared no better since soft residential construction dulled the appetite for glass and wood glues and managed to drown out sturdier roofing demand. Meanwhile, the electronics-focused business in China paused exports for what executives think will be 1 or 2 more months, pending the translation of recent design wins into tangible orders.

Across the EIMEA region, organic revenue slipped 2% on continued construction malaise, and even in Asia–Pacific, where transportation adhesives are doing their part, the gains were wiped out by solar and electronics shortfalls, leaving the region flat year-on-year. Put differently, about half of Fuller’s end markets decelerated during the quarter, which explains why volumes went nowhere after rising 2% in the first 3 months of the fiscal year.

At the same time, external factors aren’t doing the company any favors. Management says foreign-exchange headwinds alone could shave 1 to 1.5% off full-year revenue. And while 99% of what it sells in the U.S. is made in the U.S., and 96% of what it sells in China is, well, made in China, that doesn’t shield it from the fallout of tariffs or rerouted supply chains. Such indirect shocks may take quarters to reveal themselves, which is why executives are guiding to muted volume for the rest of calendar 2025 and sprinkling their commentary with caution about geopolitical flashpoints and erratic construction demand.

Even so, the firm is not exactly hunkering down; it is just pacing its spend.

Capital spending in the first half came in at $65 million, down from $90 million a year ago, a sharp drop that points more to delayed projects than to scrapped ones. To hit the reiterated $150 million cap-ex target for the full year, Fuller has to push several hefty projects through the pipe in the back half, including further stages of its enterprise-wide SAP rollout and ongoing plant consolidation work. Management admits those two programs alone will keep capital intensity hovering around $40 million a year through at least 2026, underscoring that the transformation, while strategic, is also expensive. Layer on $3.6 million of restructuring and deal-related charges in the latest quarter, mostly clean-up costs tied to exiting Flooring, and it becomes clear that the income statement is still shouldering the friction that follows any big portfolio overhaul.

However, valuation doesn’t always care about current bruises, it leans on what’s next. On that front, things look a lot better. The stock trades at about 15.5x blended earnings, right in line with a fair-value mark of 15x, which, I admit, reads as unremarkable on its own.

But zoom out: Fuller’s five-year average is 18.7x, and the specialty-chemicals peer group hovers around 16x, and right now, the trailing multiple is 14.8x, and forward is 13.7x, both sitting below the norm.

The enterprise numbers echo the same story with forward EV-to-EBITDA is 8.3x and EV-to-EBIT is 11.8x, both below industry averages. Looking at the forward PEG ratio of 0.87x versus the sector median of 1.42x means that investors are paying less than a dollar for every dollar of expected earnings growth, even though the consensus is forecasting EPS gains of 7.1% in 2025, 13.7% in 2026, and 19.5% by 2027.

Further, because the growth story here isn’t about cutting corners, but about moving pieces around, it’s pushing money into faster-growing, higher-margin adhesive lines, rolling out SAP across the business, and cutting loose weaker assets. Again, the goal is fatter margins and a stronger bottom line once volume comes back.

The numbers seem to back it up with EPS projected to grow at a 15.85% clip, ahead of the company’s own history and most of its peers.

Dividends are moving, too. Management is targeting 5+% growth on top of the 8.4% already logged. They’re counting on cash flow holding steady, even with capex still running high.

Granted, none of those forecasts will matter if construction stays in the doldrums, if FX swings grow wilder, or if solar demand fails to rebound. Yet, the 80-basis-point solar drag has already moderated versus last year, suggesting the worst of the oversupply hangover may be easing. Likewise, the electronics slowdown in China could turn around once new designs start shipping, and roofing demand inside the Building Adhesive unit is still holding up, even as housing numbers stay weak, but between that, a focus on higher-margin products, and a self-funded investment plan, Fuller looks positioned to get through the slump without wrecking its bottom line.

Final Verdict

I rate H.B. Fuller stock a buy. With low-margin businesses gone, margins and cash flow rising, guidance moving up and the stock at roughly 14× forward earnings and a sub-1 PEG, I think the prospect of mid-teens EPS growth and dividend hikes outweighs the near-term volume, FX, and construction headwinds.

data.title: PLDT Continues To Miss The Market
data.description:
I am updating my previous analysis on PLDT Inc. (NYSE:PHI). I previously rated PLDT a sell for the following reasons:

  • Volumes were continuing to struggle due to the Philippines SIM Card registration requirement
  • Inflation was improving but ARPU wasn't growing
  • Management guidance suggested underperformance in new businesses and made the dividend risky.

Since then, PLDT has fallen 9% while the S&P 500 (SP500) has returned 21%.

With continued positive news out of Maya bank and a full-year wraparound on the SIM Card requirement, I was hopeful as I came back to look at PLDT. Unfortunately while a lot has changed, not much has changed with the core business. Most critically, the growth rate continues to miss the market growth rate significantly, showing something isn't working in the core business. Operating expense and capital expense reductions have been carrying cash flow so far, but that only lasts so long.

At a DCF generated price target of $17.00, 20% downside from today's pricing, I maintain my Sell rating on PLDT Inc.

Valuation

To generate a price target, I updated my ongoing DCF analysis using the following overarching assumptions:

  • 4% short-term revenue growth with weakness in core business slightly offset by growth in new business
  • 2% cost growth to account for carryforward of G&A efficiencies
  • 2% long-run growth rate in line with Philippine market and adjust down for cost base
  • 10% discount rate based on estimated WACC of 9.3%.

This DCF generated a price target of $17, 20% downside from today's pricing, and no change from my prior analysis.

Wall Street is much more optimistic at a price target of $32, although the stock has limited analyst coverage.

The quant rating is at a hold with weak growth and profitability offset by favorable valuation multiples. Given the sector doesn't have the same geographic exposure, valuation multiples are challenging to compare. This gives more credence to growth and profitability, which largely align with my analysis.

Growth Rate Shows Something Isn't Working

With legacy services nearing the bottom (11% of revenue per the chart below), I was expecting stronger revenue performance than a 1% growth. However, revenue excluding legacy was only up 2% with declines in mobile offset by growth in home.

The overall market is expected to grow at 4.9% through 2034. Not a single business in PLDT hit the market growth rate. This indicates to me that something serious is not working in the core business as the company loses market share across the portfolio.

Throughout the earnings call, management continued to laud 5G adoption. This makes sense, as the Philippines still has exceptionally low 5G adoption relative to other markets.

Certainly 5G adoption is ramping up rapidly (81% year-on-year), and total data traffic increased 6%. But this ramp-up has been going on for two years now and there has been no corresponding increase in ARPU or overall mobile revenue to suggest the company has a plan to monetize 5G.

Cost Base Can't Sustain Growth Forever

With revenue stagnating, cost efficiencies on both the operating expense and capital expense side have been sustaining free cash flow and the dividend to this point. S,G,&A costs were down ~10% year-over-year despite higher than normal inflation, but you can only cut so far in a telecom business with its high need for sales and infrastructure support.

The business has also brought capital intensity down from 40%+ in 2023 to 20% in the latest quarter.

20% would be theoretically sustainable based on comparable companies like Telkom Indonesia. Notice, though, that PLDT has historically been a market leader, in a bad way, in capital intensity. This tells me two things. First, they are not getting any kind of a return on investment. And two, if the strategy continues to require 5G and fiber adoption, how does cutting capital in half sustain that strategy?

Upside Potential

With legacy services largely at the bottom, the biggest upside potential comes from rightsizing growth in the core business. As discussed above, the core business excluding legacy is still not growing at the market rate. But if management strategies take hold and the business is able to meet or even exceed the market it would significantly change the valuation. Given that the current strategy has been in place for more than a year with no significant change, I do not give this a high probability.

The second, and more likely upside, is that Maya bank continues to succeed and generates outsize growth becoming a larger share of revenue and operating income. This shifts the mix away from telecom and the related high investment which could restructure the income statement and the balance sheet. Currently the business is not seeing this level of traction to significantly overtake the telecom business but it is something to keep an eye on.

Verdict

Inflation is down, SIM Card registration laws have had a full-year wraparound, market growth is forecast at close to 5%, and 5G adoption is on the upswing. With all of this, you would easily expect PLDT to be growing, but the business continues to stagnate. Even with legacy services down to 11% of total revenue, topline growth fell nearly 4 points short of the overall market.

Cost efficiencies on both operating expense and capital expense have sustained the business so far, but that has limits. While there is upside potential if management strategies to grow mobile take hold, or if Maya bank supercharges growth and restructures the company, these same strategies have been in place for over a year with limited success.

At a DCF generated price target of $17.00, 20% downside from today's pricing, I maintain my Sell rating on PLDT Inc.

data.title: Victory Munder Mid-Cap Core Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Philosophy

The Victory Munder Mid-Cap Core Growth Fund invests in mid-cap stocks that we believe have strong growth prospects and reasonable valuations. We believe this combination of fundamental strength and attractive valuation positions the Fund for strong competitive performance. These characteristics have historically contributed to the Fund’s successful long-term record, and we are confident they should continue to serve our investors well.

Market Overview

The Victory Munder Mid-Cap Core Growth Fund A-Shares (MUTF:MGOAX)(without sales charge) outperformed its Russell Midcap®Index benchmark by 1 basis point (bps) in the first quarter. The outperformance came entirely from stock selection. On a sector basis, stock selection was most positive in Health Care and Technology and most negative in Industrials and Energy.

The Russell Midcap®Index benchmark saw significant style dispersion in the first quarter, with Growth stocks underperforming Value stocks. Mid-cap stocks overall fared better than large-caps, with the Russell Midcap®Index outperforming the Russell 3000®Index. The Fund’s positive style factor exposure to profitability was the largest contributor to performance. And despite broad weakness in growth stocks, the Fund’s second largest style contributor was our positive exposure to growth. We attribute this to our focus on companies with strong growth and profitability metrics combined with reasonable valuations.

The Fund’s strongest stock selection came in the Health Care and Technology sectors. In Health Care, Intra-Cellular Therapies was our strongest contributor. The company makes drugs treating schizophrenia and depression. Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) announced in January it would acquire Intra-Cellular for a 39% premium. We sold our position soon after the announcement. In Technology, our strongest contributor was OSI Systems (OSIS). The maker of security screening systems reported strong orders and a record backlog in its fiscal second quarter and raised full-year guidance. More broadly, the Technology sector was the worst-performing sector in the Russell Midcap®Index in the first quarter, but the Fund’s Technology holdings held up better in the speculative growth sell-off late in the quarter.

Stock selection lagged in Industrials and Energy. Within Industrials, our worst contributor was Parsons Corporation (PSN), which provides government technology solutions. Fourth quarter results were negatively impacted by charges on two large projects, and guidance for the coming year implies a material deceleration in revenue growth. We sold our position. In Energy, our worst detractor was Matador Resources (MTDR). The oil & gas producer reported in-line fourth quarter results in February. However, Matador sold off in late February and early March as oil prices fell. We sold our position for better opportunities.

On an individual stock basis, our best contributors were Intra-Cellular and Halozyme Therapeutics (HALO), while our worst detractors were Evercore Inc. (EVR) and Parsons. Halozyme partners with major pharmaceutical companies, using its core ENHANZE technology to allow subcutaneous drug administration. The company raised its 2025 and multi-year guidance in early January, as ENHANZE continues to grow rapidly and expand its partnerships. The investment banking advisory firm Evercore sold off as tariff headlines created uncertainty for dealmaking. Evercore was one of our strongest contributors in 2024 as it continued to gain share from larger peers like UBS and Barclays.

At quarter-end, the Fund is overweight in the Financials and Health Care sectors and underweight in Industrials, Materials, and Utilities.

Overall, the Fund’s market capitalization is roughly 6% higher than the benchmark on a weighted-average market-cap basis. The Fund continues to have positive exposures to growth and profitability.

data.title: New All-Time Highs, But The Consumer Is Not Buying It
data.description:
Close but no cigar! The S&P 500 briefly traded above levels that would have registered a new all-time high, but fell below it minutes before the closing bell. Interest rates eased across the curve in expectation that we will see at least two rate cuts from the Federal Reserve this year. The dollar continued to fall to what is now a three-year low. Volatility has collapsed as the fears that rocked the market less than three months ago have evaporated. The conflict between Iran and Israel is now behind us. Investors seem certain that trade tensions will continue to de-escalate, and tariffs will have minimal impact on inflation or the economy at large.

My concern is that the market has run ahead of the fundamentals that dictate long-term value. I continue to think that the market’s advance this week is in anticipation of another extension of the 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs that expires on July 8. At that point, we are going to need a new catalyst for this market to keep climbing. As valuations increase to historically high levels, reflecting confidence in the economic outlook, the rate of growth is slowing to a dangerously low rate. While investors are buying stocks, consumers are not buying as many of the goods and services that account for approximately 70% of our economic output.

In its second revision, the rate of economic growth for Q1 2025 was lowered again from a contraction of 0.2% to 0.5%. While the trade imbalance, due to a surge in imports to avoid tariffs, was the reason for the contraction, there was a troubling adjustment to the most influential component of our growth rate. Consumer spending growth (PCE) was revised down from 1.2% to just 0.5%, which was largely the result of slower service sector spending. That is the weakest pace of growth since the pandemic which occurred before the trade war began. While spending picked up to start the second quarter, as consumers looked to front run price increases from tariffs, the estimated rate of growth has steadily declined from a peak of 4% to 1.9%. I think it will be revised even lower after we see this morning’s report for personal spending in May. A softer rate of growth should be expected during the middle of this economic cycle, but it is getting extremely soft.

While the trade war damaged consumer sentiment, which is weighing on spending, the labor market is becoming an additional factor. Initial claims for jobless benefits fell 10,000 last week to 236,000, while the four-week moving average held steady at 245,000. The weak spot is in continuing claims, which rose to 1.97 million, indicating that those who lose their jobs are having a more difficult time finding new ones. That is the highest number since November 2021

Forecasts for corporate profits in the second quarter have come down more than we typically see during the typical quarter, but still result in year-over-year growth. Guidance for the second half of this year will be more important. If the rate of economic growth continues to deteriorate, as I expect, it will be very difficult to see the S&P 500 meet expectations for accelerating rates of earnings and revenue growth. That is a negative rate of change that is likely to limit the upside in the major market indexes after we crest the new all-time high in the index last set on February 19.

data.title: Republic Services: Amazing Growth Story, But Not Worth Paying Big-Tech Prices
data.description:
Republic Services (NYSE:RSG) has had an amazing performance both when it comes to the stock price (up 520% in the past 10 years) and the revenue, cash flow and any other metric we can think of. This solid compounding with margins that compete even with some tech companies has led to an overall quite high valuation, with key valuation ratios higher than those of giants like Alphabet's, leading to my Hold rating as I wait for a better entry level.

Introduction & Financials

Republic Services is the second-largest U.S. waste removal company, being a very solid compounder and an example of an amazing stock growth story since going public nearly three decades ago. They provide a very essential service, and ~35% of it is owned by one of Bill Gates' companies (Cascade Investment), who's known for investing in this type of vital companies.

As we can see even in the overview above, RSG benefits from acquiring smaller players and achieving economies of scale, and continues to see opportunities for consolidation, offering strong and very solid cash flows through a national footprint of vertically integrated operations.

Their margins are actually very solid, competing even with some tech companies, so they really managed to turn waste into quite a treasure. This is also the result of several accretive M&As, especially in recent years, as I will mention below.

The FCF compounded very well over time, with 5Y average FCF growth rates of as much as 17.26%, which is exceptional for what is otherwise a slow and steady industry, and this has been quite a solid growth story so far, with the stock up >1,300% since 1998 (without accounting for dividends)! However, on a ~$76 billion market cap, we are looking at $2.35 billion in the past 12 months, placing them at a ratio of ~32.3, which is actually higher than Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) 27.4! This implies that the market has very high expectations about RSG, which may be really tough to justify in the long term. Remember that just because they did well in the past, it doesn't guarantee that such levels of growth will continue. Even the forward expected FCF ratios are well below their recent averages, as you can see above.

Not only is it a high ratio for them, but it's actually significantly higher than the industry median. There is likely a premium for their quality and growth history, but that's a high price to pay for these cash flows compared to the industry's median, which is at less than half their current P/FCF ratio.

And it's not like this company is debt-free. There is significant long-term debt ($12.86 billion) generating ~$540 million in interest per year so far. Financially, based on the latest report, they are not in such a good position, with current assets well below their current liabilities – meaning they likely need to issue short-term debt to fund operations – and quite a lot of debt. On the bright side, repaying it combined with rate cuts could lead to higher cash flows, so we could see another ~10% quite easily if they focus on that for a couple of years and rates get cut further. However, those liabilities are not properly backed by real assets, as half of their total assets is actually in goodwill that they acquired through past acquisitions, which is not an asset they can sell if needed. They can, however, sell the companies/subsidiaries behind this goodwill, but that's basically reverting their growth. So, even then, the equity is well below their $76.5 billion market cap, placing the P/B ratio at a very high level of 6.6.

Still, Waste Management (WM), their largest competitor, is actually even worse in this regard (and others, such as the growth and margins), with a P/B of ~10.6 and nearly double RSG's debt, so at least they're not the most expensive option from this point of view.

Dividends & Buybacks

With 21 years of consecutive dividend increases, RSG is close to becoming a Dividend Aristocrat. This could be another potential catalyst for stock price appreciation, as it could bring more attention to the stock and create a bit more demand. But, remember that at such a high valuation, they can't really pay significant yields. Currently, they pay a less than 1% dividend and ramped up the buybacks to ~$500 million per year (at all-time highs), bringing the total payout ratio to around ~54% based on the TTM cash flow for a ~1.65% combined yield. It's not a very high payout, but they simply can't pay attractive yields due to the valuation, the only noteworthy potential being the long-term dividend/buyback growth.

Risks & Opportunities

In recent years, on top of CPI escalators which offer inflation protection to their revenue (making them a very attractive defensive investment from this point of view), Republic Services has made several acquisitions that made it achieve those incredible levels of growth and solidify its position as the second-largest provider of waste disposal services in the U.S.

Since 2021, the company made 5 acquisitions so far, with their U.S. Ecology purchase in 2022 being worth as much as $2.2 billion and expected to deliver immediate FCF accretion, as well as $40 million in annual synergies following the first 3 years. This kind of deal ($4.8 billion invested in acquisitions in the past 3 years) and efficient capital allocation is what helped them increase the dividend for 21 consecutive years and accelerate the M&As in recent years at the same time.

Another advantage is the industry's high barriers of entry (such as the logistic network behind it, need for permitting, etc.), which makes industry consolidation even more beneficial for the company, as it limits the competition even more, while seeing it replaced with new players is hard.

However, the brief Liberation Day ~9% crash (as defensive as this company is) shows something very important about the company. They are tied to the U.S. economy, which is why the stock is still weak to issues, slowdowns, recessions, etc. – potentially even opening an opportunity to buy. However, given its weakness to the economy and very high valuation, well above their and the industry's averages, there's even fewer reasons to call this a buy at these prices.

Still, especially given their debt position and CPI escalators, rate cuts should be quite beneficial – especially since there are hopes for cuts as early as July. Not only would they potentially allow inflation to rise a bit, lower their cost of debt and interest – potentially making room for even more acquisitions at better costs – but they would also boost the economy by increasing disposable budgets, spending, and, of course, the need for their waste removal services.

They also have ambitious sustainability goals, targeting a focus on biogas and recycling by 2030. This is in line with long-term tailwinds, and should continue to help the company see demand and revenue growth, expecting $120 million incremental EBITDA annually by 2029 from their landfill gas to energy partnerships, as well as over $220 million in annual revenue from their polymer centers.

Another opportunity, especially with such a high valuation compared to the industry that allows them to issue stock and buy others very conveniently, is the potential international expansion. For example, the company has a small footprint in Canada, but could expand it significantly over time through acquisitions. They already have a solid business model, so their task would be to apply and adapt it to the markets they expand to. Still, they did not announce a clear plan for that, so for now, it's just a potential catalyst that could come along the way, not something guaranteed.

Valuation

For the valuation, I'll be using a DCF where we start with a FCF of $2.35 billion and assume 8% annual growth for the 5 five years, followed by 5% for the next 5, to account for potential M&A ramp-ups and rate cuts in the following years boosting their growth – plus a 2% terminal growth rate. For a 6.5% WACC, which I think is reasonable for a mature, regulated infrastructure business with solid and growing cash flows, and is in line with estimates. With ~$12.8 billion in net debt and ~312.5 million shares outstanding, the DCF model gets us to an enterprise value of ~$77.5 billion, an equity value of ~$64.7 billion, and an estimated intrinsic value per share of roughly $207, suggesting that the stock is likely modestly overvalued by ~15–20% compared to the result.

Conclusions

Although I'm really not a fan of their financial health due to the overall weak balance sheet, Republic Service remains a vital company for the U.S., and for now, one that, I believe, is a Hold as I wait for better prices.

If they drop to ~15–20x the FCF, meaning a price of roughly $112.75 to $150.35, then this would get much closer to a Buy for me. I'd expect such levels to come perhaps during a recession or other significant issues for the country. That would imply one of their lowest ratios, not very far from the industry's median. From that level, they could afford to pay a better dividend, and the upside would be much more significant if they continue to grow, consolidate and perhaps even develop internationally one day.

data.title: Watch 5 Of Warren Buffett's Best Berkshire 'Safer' June Dividend Dogs Of 35
data.description:
Foreword

Dan Burrows says in Kiplinger Investing:

"The Berkshire Hathaway portfolio is a diverse set of blue chips and, increasingly, lesser-known growth bets. Here's a look at every stock picked by Warren Buffett and his lieutenants.

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) added no new holdings in Q1, 2025: but more than doubled its position in Constellation Brands (STZ), the beer and wine and spirit maker whose brands include Corona Extra, Modelo Especial and Robert Mondavi Winery. The holding company upped its stake by 6.4 million shares bringing its total position to 12 million shares worth $2.2 billion as of the end of Q1. At 0.85% of the portfolio, STZ is now Berkshire's 15th largest holding.

It should come as little surprise that Berkshire continued to add to some favored holdings. Buffett boosted the conglomerate's investment in Occidental Petroleum (OXY) fractionally, or by 0.29% in Q1. With 265 million shares worth $13.1 billion, OXY is Berkshire's sixth most important position. Note that OXY shares lost almost a fifth of their value last year and traded as low as $46 at one point in December. It wouldn't be the first time Buffett went bargain hunting in OXY stock, which he first bought for Berkshire in 2022.

Elsewhere, Berkshire continued to build up positions in recent additions like Domino's Pizza (DPZ) and Pool (POOL).

The holding company increased its stake in pizza chain DPZ by another 10%. With 2.6 million shares worth $1.2 billion at Q1’s end, DPZ is the portfolios 30th largest holding. Berkshire more than doubled its stake in Pool, buying another 865,311 shares, which makes it the 26th largest portfolio holding.

Berkshire also continued to add to its position in Sirius XM Holdings (SIRI). The stake further segments BRK.B’s position as SIRI’s largest stakeholder, owning 35% of its common stocks outstanding.

Dropped from the list in Q1 2025 were Citigroup, DaVita and Nu Holdings.

Any collection of stocks is more clearly understood when subjected to yield-based (dogcatcher) analysis, this Buffett/Berkshire batch is perfect for the dogcatcher process. Here are the June data for 26 dividend paying stocks in the Kiplinger and YChart-documented collection of 35 now owned by the Berkshire-Hathaway firm as of 6/24/25. Updated quarterly per BRK SEC filings, the next report is due in Mid-August.

Over five years ago the rapid market recovery, following the Ides of March 2020 Covid plunge, made the possibility of owning productive dividend shares from the Buffett collection out of reach for first-time investors.

This June 2025 update shows that two of the top ten dogs of Berkshire stocks by yield now live up to the dogcatcher ideal of paying annual dividends (from a $1K investment) exceeding their single share prices:

The Kraft Heinz Co (KHC) and Sirius XM; are the two IDEAL dividend fetchers.

[See a summary of top ten fair-priced May Berkshire Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and the five “safer” ideal-priced to watch and wait for in the Afterword, at the tail of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 15.07% To 31.06% Net Gains From Top-Ten Berkshire Dogs By June, 2026

Seven of ten top Berkshire Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this June, 2025 yield-based forecast, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 70% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding Berkshire stocks and the median of one-year target prices from analysts, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to June, 2026 were:

Constellation Brands was projected to net $314.58 based on the median of target prices estimated by 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 30% less than the market as a whole.

Kraft Heinz was projected to net $269.29, based on the median of target price estimates from 21 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 73% less than the market as a whole.

Diageo (DEO) was projected to net $254.23 based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 7 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 42% less than the market as a whole.

Occidental Petroleum was projected to net $187.27, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 26 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 13% less than the market as a whole.

Louisiana-Pacific (LPX) was projected to net $181.97, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 10 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 89% greater than the market as a whole.

T-Mobile US (TMUS) was projected to net $176.67, based on the median of target price estimates from 27 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 36% less than the market as a whole.

Chevron (CVX) was projected to net $176.62, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 23 analysts, less broker fees.The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 17% under the market as a whole.

Apple (AAPL) was projected to net $137.98 based on the median of target price estimates from 40 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 22% over the market as a whole.

Coca-Cola (KO) was projected to net $127.16, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 54% under the market as a whole.

Lennar (LEN) was projected to net $110.96, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 15 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 36% more than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 19.37% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten Berkshire stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 12% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

35 Berkshire Holdings By Analyst Target Gain Estimates

The June 2025 Berkshire 35 By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top June Berkshire Stocks By Yield Ranged 2.23% To 6.15% Per YCharts

Top ten Berkshire Dogs occupied four of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Four consumer staple stalwarts, placed first, fourth, sixth and eighth, Kraft Heinz [1], Diageo [4], Coca-Cola [6], and Constellation [8].

The lone communication services dog placed second, Sirius XM Holdings [2].

Third place was one of two energy dogs, Chevron Corp [3]. The other energy member placed ninth, Occidental Petroleum [9].

Finally, three financial stocks placed seventh fifth, and tenth: Ally Financial [5], Jefferies Financial (JEF) [7], and Bank of America (BAC) [10], to complete the June Dogs of Berkshire by yield.

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The pie chart above shows the relative strengths of the top ten June Berkshire dogs by dividend and price as of market close 6/24/2025.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share price. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by two of the highest yield Dogs of BRK.

Kraft Heinz, and Sirius XM, both lived up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices.

However, five more, Jefferies, Ally Financial, Bank of America, Occidental Petroleum, and, Coca-Cola are within striking distance and show prices within $30 of their fair dividend dollar price.

26 Berkshire Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 6/24/25

The lower chart reveals the rest of the 26 Berkshire-held Dividend dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the June Berkshire batch was Kraft Heinz at $16.27 per dividend dollar and the most expensive was Heico (HEI) at $1322.75 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Three of Twenty-Six BRK Dividend Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Three Berkshire stocks so infected are Coca-Cola, Jefferies Financial, and Bank of America. These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, trade deals, war, and tariffs.

Price Drops, Dividend Increases, or Stock Splits Could Get All Ten Berkshire Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, two in the top ten are already ideally priced. Beside those two, already in the ideal zone, seven more top-yield stocks are within 44% of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise their dividends or do stock splits to lower prices. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 10.81% To 29.98% Top Ten Berkshire Upsides To June, 2026; (32) Five -1.16% to -19% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 8.76% Disadvantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Berkshire Dogs As of June, 2026

Ten-top Berkshire dogs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten Berkshire dogs selected 6/24/25 by yield, represented four of eleven sectors. Consumer Discretionary, Health Care, Industrials, Materials, Real Estate, Technology, and Utilities missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Berkshire Dogs (34) To Deliver 15.13% Vs. (35) 16.58% Net Gains by All Ten Come June 24, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Berkshire Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analysts to deliver 8.76% LESS gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The tenth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, STZ, showed the top analyst-estimated gain of 31.046%.

The five lowest-priced Berkshire top-yield dogs for June were: Sirius XM; Kraft Heinz; Ally; Occidental; Bank of America, with prices ranging from $22.41 to $46.66.

Five higher-priced Berkshire top-yield dogs as of June 24 were: Jefferies, Coca-Cola; Diageo; Chevron; Constellation, with prices ranging from $55.34 to $164.49.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are, historically, only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As parade and No King euphoria fades, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential deals will gain momentum into 2025. So, with the exception of the few stocks and T-bills, look for at least half of the ten BRK Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly.

Afterword

Watch 5 ‘Safer' June Berkshire DiviDogs Become Ideal this Summer

Late-June 2025,16 of the 35 BRK were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 6/24/25 data showed Ally the closest to fair-priced and 4 others in queue,

How BRK 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce “Fair Price” Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that no dividend from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend BRK dogs, conforms to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or less than) the price of a single share.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock; a so-called “fair price.”

A market correction of 84% is required to put all ten “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or do stock splits to bring shares the dogcatcher ideal level. That’s a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

These top five net gainers are led by the closest IDEAL “Safer” Berkshire buy in-waiting, Ally Financial. The top five await the pending sell-off: After Ally price drops below $34.64, Kroger price will drop below $35.77, then Louisiana Pacific shares will plunge to $33.46, Chevron shares will be buyable at $82.70, Capital One shares will be bumble at $48.99 and T-Mobil will be fair game at $59.33.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Berkshire Updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnote

BRK Holdings Per SEC Form 13F as of 3/31/24

(Q1 2025 Report)

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Berkshire dividend dog stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

data.title: Income Built On How America Lives, Works, And Spends: Yields +7.5%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities.

It has been two years since the COVID-19 pandemic was officially declared to be over. In recent months, we have seen a steady increase in the number of companies instituting “Return To Office” policies, and less than 20% of LinkedIn job postings are for remote or hybrid work arrangements. Even if we were to look outside, at the in-person vs. virtual office setup, the reality is that not every business can operate from a kitchen table or a corner coffee shop. A dentist still needs their clinic, auto shops need a bay, and data centers need physical infrastructure. Warehouses, apartment complexes, hospitals, retail centers – these aren’t relics of the past; they’re the backbone of a functioning society.

At the same time, the use of cash for transactions continues to decline steadily at a global level. The pandemic accelerated the process, but consumers still swipe, tap, and click. In the United States, only about 15% of all payments are made with cash. Whether it’s a vacation booked online, groceries ordered for delivery, or dining at a restaurant, spending keeps the economic engine running, and this spending is made possible by access to credit and the supporting infrastructure. Source.

The beauty of income investing is that you don’t need to predict the next big thing – you just need to invest in what keeps the world turning. In that spirit, let us look at our top picks.

Pick #1: RQI – Yield 7.8%

Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI) offers diversified access to the resilient REIT sector through an active management strategy that selects leaders. The CEF (Closed-End Fund) houses 193 holdings, but has some of the best-in-class REITs across sectors occupying its top ten positions. These represent ~48.5% of the fund’s market value. Source.

About 82% of RQI’s investments are REIT common equity, while 18% represent preferred securities, providing modest exposure to the fixed-income space. Notably, the CEF’s top focus areas are those where tenants sign long-term leases and are less likely to move frequently – Data Centers (8%), Healthcare (13%), Telecommunications (14%), and Industrial (7%) properties.

RQI uses 29% leverage to amplify the returns from its active strategy. About 81% of the borrowings carry fixed interest rates at ~2% for a weighted average term of 2.4 years.

On Seeking Alpha, we have encountered several authors’ perspectives over the years, claiming that leveraged CEFs are bad investments. An investment isn’t bad simply because it is a CEF or because it operates with leverage. Let us take a look at the performance of RQI against peer CEFs, and the popular passive, un-leveraged REIT index, the Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ).

In this list, RQI and JRS operate with the highest ~29% leverage, arguably delivering the strongest total returns despite the record pace of interest rate hikes. Neuberger Berman Real Estate Securities Income Fund (NRO) operates with a lower leverage of 19.2%, coming next in line, followed by Cohen & Steers Total Return Realty Fund (RFI) that operates with no leverage. Notably, all these actively managed funds outperform VNQ, by far.

RQI pays $0.08/month, reflecting a 7.8% yield. The REIT industry continues to operate on sound fundamentals, and we expect RQI to experience tailwinds from rate cuts, given its strategic allocation to lucrative sub-sectors with excellent growth potential and predictable rental income.

Pick #2: SYF Preferreds – Up To 8.1% Yields

Synchrony Financial (SYF) is the largest issuer of store-branded credit cards in the U.S. These cards are typically offered through partnerships between retailers and banks, allowing customers to earn rewards that are primarily usable within the retail brand’s ecosystem. The model is highly profitable for all three parties involved – retailers, issuers, and consumers. For retailers, it boosts brand loyalty and customer retention. For issuers like SYF, the cards come with low customer acquisition costs, high interest rates, and extended customer lifetime value. For consumers, the appeal often lies in attractive rewards in their favorite retailer’s ecosystem and the absence of annual fees.

During Q1, SYF reported having engaged with ~70 million customers, generating $41 billion in purchase volume. SYF has the highest number of cardholders in the U.S., and the bank added or renewed more than 10 partners in the first quarter, including significant renewals with Ashley (nearly 15 years) and American Eagle Outfitters (nearly 30 years). The bank also secured new partnerships with Sun Country Airlines and Texas A&M University Veterinary Medical Teaching Hospital.

Notably, a significant portion (~51%) of SYF’s purchase volume comes from non-discretionary categories, such as Bill Pay, Grocery, Gas, and Health & Pet. Source.

Q1 2025 results included net earnings of $757 million or $1.89 per diluted share, and reported a tangible book value per share increase of 15% YoY. SYF also maintains a robust funding structure, with consumer checking & savings deposits representing ~84% of the total funding at the end of Q1.

During the quarter, SYF reported that it had completed its existing share repurchase plan and announced a new one for $2.5 billion. Over the past decade, SYF has repurchased almost half of its outstanding shares, demonstrating a strong focus on returning capital to shareholders. Moreover, the company also increased its regular quarterly dividend by 20% YoY to $0.30/share. Since the commencement of dividend payments in 2016, the bank has delivered regular raises to shareholders, and its current annualized yield stands at 2.1%, at a modest 13% payout ratio. SYF maintains a robust balance sheet, recently upgraded to BBB by Fitch as a result of strong execution following the loss of top retail partners (despite concentration), and their proactive approach in response to the CFPB’s ban on excessive late fees. SYF demonstrates that it is led by an experienced management team and maintains the ability to address elevated regulatory pressures effectively.

Over the years, SYF has maintained a rather stable +30 day delinquency rate across the economic cycle, due to its ability to underwrite towards risk adjustment at the partnership level effectively.

As of Q1 2025, the bank reported that delinquency rates are trending better than expectations and near or below historical averages. 30-day delinquency was 4.52%, which is 4 bps below the bank's pre-pandemic average. SYF’s net charge-off rate was 6.38% in Q1, marking a 7 bps YoY increase, placing it 54 bps above its pre-pandemic average.

Overall, SYF demonstrates expert navigation through economic changes and represents a well-managed banking institution with a rather simple business model. The bank has two non-cumulative perpetual preferreds, costing them $21 million every quarter. This compares to a $97 million common dividend expense for Q1, and $757 million in net earnings. The preferreds enjoy 36x coverage from the bank’s earnings, and their dividends are considered qualified for tax purposes.

  • 5.625% Fixed Rate Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock, Series A (SYF.PR.A) – Yield 7.6%

  • 8.250% Fixed Rate-Reset Non-Cumulative Perpetual Preferred Stock, Series B (SYF.PR.B) – Yield 8.1%.

Currently, SYF-A is a well-covered fixed-rate preferred offering a 7.5% yield and ~33% upside to par.

SYF-B is a rate-reset preferred with a high base rate of 4.044%. Notably, this security only floats after four years, making it a solid option for a high 8.1% yield for at least that period. Given its high coupon, this is a likely candidate for being called when eligible.

Conclusion

Every day, trillions of dollars exchange hands in global markets. From education to job creation, from corporate expansion to the groceries in your cart – money flows through every corner of human activity. We’ve built a society where progress, comfort, and even basic necessities are powered by capital. With RQI, you can tap into the long-term potential of real estate assets, and SYF-A lets you collect your cut of the billions in transaction volume supported by this well-capitalized financial institution offering.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we tap into the massive global markets to draw our commission in the form of dividends and interest income. We hold over 45 equity securities in our model portfolio and a comprehensive fixed-income portfolio of preferred stock and baby bonds. We’ve built a powerful engine for strong, recurring income – One designed to fuel lasting financial independence.

In a world where money never stops moving, we simply position ourselves to get paid along the way. This is the beauty of our Income Method.

data.title: First Advantage: Enlarged TAM, Good Execution, Early Signs Of Macro Recovery (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on First Advantage (FA) in Nov ’24, in which I recommended a hold rating as I did not fancy the merger between FA and Sterling Check, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. I am pleasantly surprised by how well FA has executed this acquisition and integration, and with the valuation now at a more palatable level, I have turned bullish. I upgrade my rating to buy.

Solid execution so far

A key part of my hold rating previously was the leverage profile post the acquisition (pro forma 4.4x). Given that this is a business with inherent cyclicality, I felt that was a big overhang on the stock valuation. But over the past 7 months, FA has demonstrated their focus on tackling this. First of all, management raised their cost synergy target to $65–80 million, up from $60–70 million, of which $37 million of run-rate synergies were already actioned by 1Q25, well ahead of schedule. This gives me a lot of confidence that integration is tracking better than expected. Secondly, FA has prepaid $20 million of debt in just the last few months and reiterated its goal of returning to a 2–3x net leverage range.

These figures may not seem like a lot at face value, but remember this was achieved in just a few months after the acquisition closed—which generally is the period where things are messy and still in the planning stages. If FA can achieve this in just a few months, I am optimistic that FA can continue to execute and achieve its net leverage target.

Moreover, the recent results make it clear business is accelerating. 4Q24 saw a record number of new logo wins, and 1Q25 saw another quarter of record in total bookings. Retention also held steady at 96%, which tells me customers are not only staying, but they are adopting more solutions. What’s even more encouraging is the early proof of revenue synergies. Management has already identified $400 million in cross-sell opportunities within just its top 150 accounts. Historically, these types of cross-sell deals have a >90% success rate, which means FA has very good visibility into this incremental $400 million revenue. For reference, $400 million is ~27% of FY24 pro forma revenue.

Digital Identity Helps Expand the TAM

An important development that I think has dramatically shifted FA's long-term growth outlook is the push into digital identity verification. To put it into perspective, this pivot has expanded FA’s TAM by more than two times, from $10 billion to $24 billion. And I expect a significant surge in demand for digital identity verification. As much as AI has helped improve productivity, it has also resulted in a lot of fraudulent identity cases (like AI-generated deepfakes and fraudulent applicants). This is a growing concern, and the fact that management noted it’s now a dominant topic in C-suite discussions further validates my view that there is latent demand for this product.

From a value proposition perspective, this move upstream in the hiring funnel makes a lot of sense for FA. By doing so, FA can better help clients to avoid the cost and risk of progressing these fraudulent actors through their recruiting pipelines. This value proposition is already resonating, as FA now has >3,000 customers that have adopted the solution.

From a business and stock perspective, the merits of this move go beyond just larger TAM and better value prop, but it has structurally diversified FA’s business away from hiring cycles and into secular demand for risk management. Notably, this new revenue stream carries a high margin and is recurring. Put together, this move itself should justify FA trading at a structurally higher multiple.

First Green Shoots of a Macro Recovery

Besides the acquisition, I was also concerned about the macro weakness, which I expected to weigh heavily on base hiring volumes. While it is too early to call a full recovery, I believe things are turning around. Management noted that the stabilization they saw in 1Q25 has continued into April and May. This can be validated by third-party labor data that showed sequential improvement in hiring activity last month. Although the narrative is that businesses are still uncertain about the macro outlook, the point here is that there has evidently been a shift in sentiment, from “negative momentum” to “stabilization.” This sentiment shift matters a lot for FA because it provides a floor for the most cyclical part of the business.

Valuation

With a better-than-expected execution, larger TAM, and early signs of macro conditions turning around, I am now more open to valuing FA on a long-term basis. The new FY28 guidance calls for an adj. EPS range between $1.65 and $2, marking a ~22% CAGR at the midpoint. This is a plausible target, as the business is growing off a low base and has inherent operating leverage and high financial leverage. So, what should be the multiple that FA should trade if it can achieve this guidance? Historically, FA trades at an average of 16.5x NTM PE. I believe FA should trade at a premium to this because of its larger scale, bigger TAM, structurally less cyclical business profile, and earnings growth outlook. I would think FA should trade at 18x, as Robert Half Inc. (RHI) trades at 18x with just a mid-single-digit earnings growth outlook. At 18x, FA shares could be worth ~$33.

Risk

The biggest risk is that macro conditions turn for the worse, which could happen despite the early green shoots. In that scenario, the stock will very likely get negatively impacted, as the large chunk of FA’s business is still cyclical—tied to base hiring activity. That would also make it hard for FA to continue its current momentum in deleveraging its balance sheet, which adds another layer of pressure to the stock.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my rating for FA is an upgrade to buy. My previous concerns regarding post-merger execution and leverage have been substantially de-risked. FA is not only integrating Sterling well but is also unlocking a new TAM that fundamentally reshapes the long-term outlook. With early signs of a cyclical macro recovery providing a floor and a clearer path toward significant earnings growth, I believe the current valuation is attractive.

data.title: Victory RS Mid Cap Growth Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly Highlights

  • The Victory RS Mid Cap Growth Fund (MUTF:RSMOX)(Class A Shares, without sales charge) returned -11.50% for the three months ended March 31, 2025, underperforming the Russell Midcap® Growth Index,1 which returned -7.12%.
  • Fund performance relative to the benchmark was negatively impacted by stock selection in the Technology sector; underperformance was partially offset by stock selection in the Consumer Discretionary sector.
  • This period’s absolute performance was hindered in part by the relative performance of secular mid-cap growth stocks as mid growth, as defined by the Russell Midcap® Growth Index, underperformed mid-cap value, as defined by the Russell Midcap® Value Index,2 -7.12% vs. -2.11%.
  • We continue to believe technology-oriented companies are better positioned for the longer term, especially following the massive “test run” of technology-aided solutions people have leveraged to work, shop, and communicate from home, which we believe has permanently sped up the adoption of these new technologies at the expense of legacy products and services.
  • Mid-cap growth stocks have outperformed their mid-cap value counterparts over 3, 10 and 15 years as of March 31, 2025, per Russell.

Market Performance / Fundamentals Snapshot

Index returns are for illustrative purposes only and do not represent actual Fund performance. Index performance does not reflect management fees, transaction costs or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Market Commentary

After two consecutive years of impressive 20%-plus annual returns for the broad market (as measured by the S&P 500®Index3), the bellwether large-cap index shed 4.3% during the first quarter. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite fared even worse, losing 10.3% during the quarter. This was the worst performance for these indexes since the second and third quarters of 2022, respectively.

The sharp decline in stocks and subsequent elevated volatility comes as no real surprise. In fact, the pullback began in earnest in December 2024 after post-election euphoria—and the expectation for pro-business policies—was largely replaced by declining investor sentiment. Investors wondered about President Trump’s commitment to installing and enforcing a wide range of tariffs. Was this a negotiating tactic given the steep trade imbalance that the U.S. has with most other countries? Would this encourage the repatriation of supply chains and boost domestic manufacturing? Or would this type of intervention usher in a new era of elevated inflation, put the brakes on global trade, and act as a headwind for corporate profits? The market despises such uncertainty, and thus we are not entirely surprised that investors became more risk-averse during the first quarter.

Monetary policy and the trajectory of interest rates have also been pain points for U.S. investors. Tariffs are, in effect, a form of taxation, and to the extent possible, future higher costs are likely to be passed on to consumers. This could have a ripple effect in terms of inflation. The Federal Reserve has already signaled intentions to slow-roll future interest rate cuts, and if future CPI readings suggest the re-emergence of inflation, the Fed may be inclined to take an even slower path with regard to monetary policy.

In terms of investment styles, growth stocks underperformed their value counterparts during the first quarter. The uncertainties surrounding interest rates, as well as the strong run that many high-growth names had enjoyed over the past several years, made them somewhat vulnerable to a pullback. The Russell 3000® Growth Index4 declined 10.0% during the first quarter. Large-cap growth stocks, represented by the Russell 1000® Growth Index,5 also pulled back by approximately 10% during the quarter as momentum finally seemed to be waning for the “Magnificent Seven.” Growth stocks also stumbled further down the cap spectrum. The Russell Midcap®Growth Index declined 7.1% for the quarter, while small-cap growth stocks represented by the Russell 2000®Growth Index6 declined 11.1% during the same period.

Despite the turmoil and uncertainty, we like to remind investors that volatility also breeds opportunity. We think this is especially true for active managers in the growth space with a long time horizon and a commitment to a proven investment philosophy. We acknowledge that headwinds linger, and “uncertainty” appears to be the theme of the moment. Nevertheless, we see many exciting growth companies—especially among the cohort of small- and mid-cap names—that ended the first quarter with attractive relative valuations, in our opinion. Our team continues to focus on identifying potential opportunities within secular growth, and we believe that we are still in the early stages of a secular shift that is transforming how consumers, businesses, and employees interact. That trend, along with new emerging technologies (including artificial intelligence), is poised to have a significant impact in the years ahead, particularly among growth-oriented investment styles.

Investment Strategy

The Victory RS Mid Cap Growth Fund (the “Fund”) is guided by our philosophy that sustainable earnings growth drives long-term share price appreciation. Our investment process is focused on finding innovative companies whose core business, we believe, can grow from a mid-capitalization company to a large-cap company over time.

The team seeks companies with products and services that are growing organically, creating new markets or taking market share from existing companies. We are focused on finding companies whose business values can appreciate regardless of the underlying market environment. The Strategy is led by the team’s chief investment officer, Scott Tracy, along with portfolio managers Steve Bishop, Melissa Chadwick-Dunn, Chris Clark, Paul Leung, and Trevor Martin. The six co-portfolio managers, as well as two research analysts, serve as sector specialists and are supported by two associates, drawing on strong relationships with industry experts and company management teams.

Together, we conduct over 2,000 company meetings each year through in-person meetings, conference calls, and trade shows. We then back up our findings through discussions with industry leaders and third-party sources. We are long-term investors and seek to establish definable “anchor points,” which are quantifiable metrics that help determine a company’s potential long-term growth trajectory. Anchor points arise from our analysis of a company’s long-term capabilities and performance goals over three to five years. These long-term anchor points serve as guideposts to help us measure a company’s progress as it executes its business strategy, regardless of what is taking place in the overall market, and help prevent distractions caused by short-term stock price movements and inevitable market volatility.

Performance Review

The Victory RS Mid Cap Growth Fund (Class A Shares, without sales charge) returned -11.50% for the three months ended March 31, 2025, underperforming the Russell Midcap® Growth Index, which returned -7.12%. Fund performance relative to the benchmark was negatively impacted by stock selection in the Technology sector; underperformance was partially offset by stock selection in the Consumer Discretionary sector. This period’s absolute performance was hindered in part by the relative performance of secular mid-cap growth stocks as mid growth, as defined by the Russell Midcap® Growth Index, underperformed mid-cap value, as defined by the Russell Midcap® Value Index, - 7.12% vs. -2.11%. We continue to believe technology-oriented companies are better positioned for the longer term, especially following the massive “test run” of technology-aided solutions people have leveraged to work, shop, and communicate from home, which we believe has permanently sped up the adoption of these new technologies at the expense of legacy products and services. Mid-cap growth stocks have outperformed their mid-cap value counterparts over 3, 10 and 15 years as of March 31, 2025, per Russell.

Top Detracting Sector: Technology

The largest detractor from performance within the Technology sector was Trade Desk (1.39% ending weight), a cloud-based ad- buying platform. While the Connected Television (CTV) market continues to be strong, Trade Desk (the leader in the market) underperformed primarily because of two idiosyncratic reasons: 1) slower-than-anticipated rollout of its Kokai software upgrade, and 2) larger-than-anticipated disruption from its annual corporate restructuring. These events, coupled with renewed fears around competition from Amazon’s demand-side platform (Amazon DSP), pressured the stock in the quarter. While we are monitoring the evolving macro impacts on the advertising market, we believe that Trade Desk has largely addressed its internal issues, that Kokai adoption will catch up to internal expectations, and that competition is unchanged. We continue to hold a position.

Top Contributing Sector: Consumer Discretionary

One of the largest drivers of relative outperformance within the Consumer Discretionary sector was BJ’s Wholesale Club Holdings, Inc. (1.30% ending weight), an operator of membership warehouse clubs offering a variety of grocery, retail and home goods. BJ’s has benefited from very strong quarterly earnings results stemming from a cost-conscious consumer looking for bulk items at value prices. BJ’s has also continued to employ a nationwide new store opening strategy that has fueled strong secular growth over the long term. We continue to hold the position.

Market and Strategy Outlook

We remain cautiously optimistic about the health of the domestic economy and are excited about the prospect that the Federal Reserve has finally begun to meaningfully cut their benchmark rate to a more accommodative stance. U.S. employment remains strong, and the outlook for solid earnings growth in 2025 remains in place. While the full impact on the economy and employment from the recently announced tariffs is as yet unclear, a slightly slower pace in economic activity, if coupled with solid consumer and business spending, could augur a very favorable backdrop for growth stocks. In addition, the potential for more deregulation, continued onshoring and re-shoring, and stronger GDP growth could make the U.S. a relative safe haven. Certain segments of the economy appear well positioned to continue to meaningfully outgrow GDP, while company valuations have become increasingly compelling for long-term investors.

In the current investment landscape, there continues to be a compelling opportunity for secular growth companies outside of the mega-cap growth category. Despite their relative underperformance since November 2020, smaller-cap secular growth companies have displayed continued strength in their underlying fundamentals. We expect this fundamental strength to persist and are optimistic that the valuation gap of smaller companies will narrow substantially with that of their larger-cap peers.

Overall, while we believe the interest rate and inflation picture is getting somewhat clearer, the engineering of a soft landing for the economy remains unclear. As such, we believe that opportunities will emerge in sectors and industries that demonstrate secular resilience through the cycle and sustained growth. We feel that secular growth companies within the growth universe are especially attractive relative to cyclicals due to their strong and consistent fundamentals and potential for outperformance.

Thank you for your continued investment.
